title: Objection Handling in B2B Sales
description: How to handle typical buyer and store-manager objections at trade outlets.

steps.title: What an objection really is
steps.content: An objection is not 'no' but 'give me more info so I can say yes'. 80% of the time, an objection hides a fear (I'll lose money, I'll be stuck with dead stock, it won't sell). Your job — not to argue, but to understand the fear and remove it with numbers. An objection is free feedback.
steps.key_takeaway: Objection = fear, not rejection. Arguing is pointless, surface the fear.

steps.title: The JCAN algorithm
steps.content: J — Join ('I understand, I'd feel the same'). C — Clarify ('What specifically is driving that concern?'). A — Answer (concrete numbers and facts, no generalities). N — Next step ('Given these terms, shall we do it?'). JCAN works on any objection. Don't jump to A until you understand the real issue.
steps.key_takeaway: Join → Clarify → Answer → Next step. Four steps, not one.

steps.title: Objection: 'No shelf space'
steps.content: Real cause: 'I don't want to remove what's already working'. Solution: show turnover data on category competitors — which SKUs move less than 1 unit/day. Propose a swap: 'Remove this, place ours — we guarantee turnover based on the neighboring account X units/week'. Take on the risk: 'If it doesn't sell in 2 weeks, we take it back'.
steps.key_takeaway: No space = replace a slow SKU, not 'shove everyone over'. Risk on us.

steps.title: Objection: 'Too expensive'
steps.content: Real cause: 'I don't see how much I'll earn'. Solution: shift the conversation from 'price' to 'margin in dollars'. Example: 'This yogurt costs $0.60 wholesale, sells for $0.90 = $0.30 margin per unit. Sells 10/day = $3/day = $90/month from one shelf facing'. Don't compare to cheap alternatives — compare to turnover.
steps.key_takeaway: Translate 'expensive' into 'how much you'll earn in dollars'. Percentages don't work.

steps.title: Objection: 'It doesn't sell'
steps.content: Real cause: often true, but the reason is placement, not the product. Solution: walk to the shelf with the buyer. Check: shelf level (maybe 'the grave'), facings (maybe 1), neighbors (maybe a cheap competitor next to it), price (store may have marked it up). 70% of cases resolve via re-merchandising.
steps.key_takeaway: Walk the shelf. 70% of 'doesn't sell' = placement problem, not product.

steps.title: Objection: 'We already have a supplier'
steps.content: Real cause: 'I'm too lazy to change what works'. Solution: don't propose replacement, propose addition. 'I'm not asking you to remove X. I'm proposing our SKU as an alternative — let them compete on the shelf. If ours sells better in a month, you decide what to keep'. Removes fear and gives the buyer control.
steps.key_takeaway: Don't replace — add. Let them compete on the shelf.

steps.title: Objection: 'I'll think about it / need to check'
steps.content: Real cause: 'I don't want to say no to your face'. Solution: don't accept 'I'll think about it'. Ask: 'Who specifically will you check with? When can we make a decision — does Thursday work?'. Book a specific return date + leave them with material (price list, deck). No date = 'I'll think' = lost account.
steps.key_takeaway: 'I'll think' without a date = 'no'. Book a specific date and leave material in their hands.

quiz.question: What's really behind an objection?
options: Fear: 'I'll lose money / it won't sell'
quiz.explanation: Objection = fear. Arguing is pointless — remove the fear with numbers.

quiz.question: What does the JCAN algorithm mean?
quiz.explanation: Four steps for handling an objection. Out-of-order = argument, not dialogue.

quiz.question: 'Too expensive' — how to handle?
options: Translate to margin in dollars, show earnings
quiz.explanation: Buyers live in dollars. Percentages are abstract. Show concrete earnings.

quiz.question: 'It doesn't sell' — first action?
options: Walk to the shelf with the buyer
quiz.explanation: 70% of 'doesn't sell' cases = merchandising problem, not product.

quiz.question: Client said 'I'll think' — what do you do?
quiz.explanation: Without a date, 'I'll think' = soft 'no'. Specific date keeps the deal alive.

manager_checklist: Rep doesn't argue, surfaces the underlying fear
Applies JCAN algorithm to every objection
On 'no space' — proposes a swap with risk absorbed
On 'too expensive' — calculates margin in dollars, not percentages
On 'doesn't sell' — walks to the shelf to check merchandising
On 'we have a supplier' — proposes addition, not replacement
On 'I'll think' — books a specific return date

kpi.metric: Share of 'I'll think' with booked return date

kpi.how_to_measure: CRM: repeat orders from objection accounts
